---
title: "Faulty Nas device rendered my data inaccessible"
date: 2012-11-30
forum: Hardware
---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-11-30
I recently bought a Sitecom MD254. Since moving my network to a new ip range I've been unable to reach the Nas via the webinterface or file sharing... I will not bother you with this long sad story... suffice to say I wouldn't advice anybody to buy this product or any other Nas solution by Sitecom!!!
The problem here is the os or filesystem on the drives... physically the disks are fine.
 
Now all I want to do is retrieve my data from the disks... it's a Raid 1 mirror.. I tried to attach the disks (one at a time, I didn't want to open my pc), run Ubuntu live from a Usb flash drive to try and retrieve my data..
 
But I'm not able to mount the drive... it says in Disks application that it's part of a Raid Array and the mount button isn't there..
 
Can anybody help me retreive my data?:confused:

---

### Post by steeldriver on 2012-11-30
If you created the LiveCD from the standard desktop edition then it won't include RAID utilities - assuming the device uses Linux software RAID you would need to install the mdadm package - if it's fakeraid then I'm not so sure but it's the dmraid package I think

You will probably need to do some command line work to assemble and mount the array - there are a couple of solid RAID experts on the forum who can guide you through better than me

However it seems like overkill if the only reason you can't access the device is it's on the old IP subnet

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-11-30
No the device is currently on the new subnet... If I remove the drives it works fine.. as soon as I: shut down, put back the drives, boot up, it's inaccessible (in either subnet).
Though I can ping it and in Windows it gets assigned a Netbiosname. 
 
Problem: I read on the Ubuntu forums that hardware Raid won't be possible to read. Untill now I assumed the Sitecom Nas used harware Raid, however the device functions running a Linux os.. At this point I'm just not sure if it's hardware or software Raid, Sitecom gives me information only sparingly and I got nowhere with them working to a solution. They liked it better just to blame me!
 
I'm so frustrated!!! I bought the device for safety, scalability (in case of new pc), simplicity of use in case of an emergency, ease to reach when I have to replace a drive. Now my data is unreachable and I wasted months with their helpdesk and running diagnostics.
 
Previously I used an internal Hardware Raid 1 Array... very reliable.. 
 
**I just want my data back!** Later I'll format the drives buy a pcie hardware Raid controller and put this Sitecom(still under warranty!)hunk of junk in the trash!
 
I found a company on the net that specializes in retrieving data from these specific devices from this specific brand, which can't be good sign!! Scandalous!
I found posts by people with the same problem (exactly!) but no solutions.
 
Any help would be greatly appreciated, I'm a relative newby on this forum... how can I reach these Raid experts?

---

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2012-11-30
what do you mean you moved your network to a new IP range? you should be able to enter the IP of the device and log into it via it's webserver configuration page.

Are you sure you can't mount the shares anymore via SAMBA or NFS? It just seems very weird that "moving your network to a new IP range" would harm the built in OS in anyway

---

### Post by steeldriver on 2012-11-30
^^^ agreed

If you're determined to go ahead and try to mount / recover from your Ubuntu Live boot, the first thing to do would be install the mdadm package - you can do that from a package manager / software center or in a terminal using

```
sudo apt-get install mdadm
```Once it's installed, you can check if there's readable software RAID superblock info with something like

```
 sudo mdadm --examine /dev/sd[bc]2
```where sd[bc]2 are the RAID partitions that you've previously identified (using fdisk -l or parted -l for example). If they look good you can attempt to assemble the array.

The RAID gurus will usually step in - just be patient, sometimes people are busy and not checking new threads

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-11-30
> **dannyboy79 said:**
> what do you mean you moved your network to a new IP range? you should be able to enter the IP of the device and log into it via it's webserver configuration page.

This is what happened:
After not using my Nas for some time I switched to a new ISP, the ISP gave me a cablemodem just a modem, where previously I had adsl with a modem/router/switch. I have a small network, 2 pc's and the nas.
To connect everything I inserted a Sweex router... previously I was using the 10.0.0.x range, the Sweex router only gives me the possibillity to use 192.168.0.x
Then I tried to get into the Nas webInterface to change the (fixed) IP, I couldn't remember the password (or could I???!).. so with the help of the support guys I resetted the Nas so that I could use the default password.. at this point I noticed that I wasn't able to reach the Nas at all via the WebInterface.. They told me to remove the disks... and I was able to access the Webinterface of the empty Nas.
 
 
> **dannyboy79 said:**
> Are you sure you can't mount the shares anymore via SAMBA or NFS? It just seems very weird that "moving your network to a new IP range" would harm the built in OS in anyway 

It does.... but maybe the problem was already there (i.e. corruption of the file system) and this, including rigorous restarting) send it over the brink.. I don't understand what happened... Though I'm a an experienced It professional, I don't have a lot of experience with Linux/Unix or these Nas solutions for home-use. So, no I'm not sure, I don't have a clear view of what is SAMBA or NFS. Babysteps please ;-) 
 
> **steeldriver said:**
>  
The RAID gurus will usually step in - just be patient, sometimes people are busy and not checking new threads
No worries and thanks for the replies so far!!
Maybe it's better to start with a full install of Ubuntu on my box? It seems to me, looking at the previous replies, that before going any further it's important to establish if this is REALLY hardware Raid we're talking about.. because to me it seems to be software Raid!

---

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2012-11-30
accessing the web interface of the NAS should be possible if the discs are in the nas or not, that's what's confusing me to no end! These types of devices run an embedded form of linux most of the time. The web interface should be accessible whatever IP it is provided, whether it's in the 192.168.0.X or 10.0.0.X range. I have reset my password (using reset button-holding down for 10 seconds) on my Western Digital My Book World Edition plenty of times without any data loss. Granted they are only 1 disk and are not in RAID.

See if you can run either

```
smbtree
```
or
```
findsmb
```
from a terminal within ubuntu

---

**Dannyboy97:**
I know it sounds crazy... but I'm not the only one!! 
[http://forum.nas-central.org/viewtopic.php?f=235&t=6403&sid=9a7251a78d5165dd73112536e2c04f77](http://forum.nas-central.org/viewtopic.php?f=235&t=6403&sid=9a7251a78d5165dd73112536e2c04f77)

smbtree 
This gives me a password prompt.. I don't know so press enter.. nothing

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ findsmb

                                *=DMB
                                +=LMB
IP ADDR         NETBIOS NAME     WORKGROUP/OS/VERSION 
---------------------------------------------------------------------
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ findsmb

                                *=DMB
                                +=LMB
IP ADDR         NETBIOS NAME     WORKGROUP/OS/VERSION 
---------------------------------------------------------------------
^[[A
^[[A
169.254.0.253   SitecomNas.local
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ findsmb

                                *=DMB
                                +=LMB
IP ADDR         NETBIOS NAME     WORKGROUP/OS/VERSION 
---------------------------------------------------------------------
192.168.0.10    SITECOMNAS     [    WORKGROUP     ]
  
So it's there.. Command Similar to netstat or nbtstat in Windows. But do you see the strange ip popping up ?? I kept on giving the command during startup of the Nas and that is what happened.... default adress? No access to the webinterface on either adress though.

          Magic : a92b4efc
        Version : 0.90.00

          State : clean

Working Devices : 2

  Spare Devices : 1

   0     0       0        0        0      removed

   2     2       8        1        2      spare   /dev/sda1

>

md127 : inactive sdf1[2](S)

unused devices: <none>

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm -A /dev/md127 /dev/sdf1

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-04
I just heared from Sitecom, they tell me to forget my data, I have to reformat the disks!! Furthermore they say they cannot be held accountable for any loss of information.. So at this point only the Ubuntu community can save me ;-)
 
This is what I want to do:
Attach a single disk from the NAS's Raid1 Array to my pc and access the data (if any) on it.
 
I'm not clear what Ubuntu is telling me.. from the things I tried, I get the idea that:
There is a Raid array MD127 the Hd is ok on the hardwarelevel.
I's inactive I how do I activate it?
When I tried to assemble (was this dangerous??) it says the drive is busy.
 
I'm scared to try playing around with commands too much... I don't want to do more damage! I would like to proceed in a safe way..Anybody?

### Post by steeldriver on 2012-12-04
Hmm... so maybe it auto-assembled? in which case maybe just try mounting it - first check it didn't auto mount as well

```
mount
```then if not make a mountpoint somewhere and try it

```
sudo mkdir /mnt/raid
sudo mount -oro /dev/md127 /mnt/raid
```

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-04

```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mount -oro /dev/md127 /mnt/raid
mount: /dev/md127: can't read superblock

```
I'm stuck again.. ;-) Sorry I'm so useless with Linux, throw me a paddle (maybe I'll know what to do with it) please!

I read somewhere something about "zeroing the superblock"... but I understood that the superblock is only important in regards to booting from an array..

Correct?

```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo  mdadm --detail /dev/md127
mdadm: md device /dev/md127 does not appear to be active.
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo  mdadm -E /dev/sdc1 | grep Event
         Events : 302

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm -Q /dev/sdc1
/dev/sdc1: is not an md array
/dev/sdc1: device 2 in 2 device undetected raid1 /dev/.tmp.md1.  Use mdadm --examine for more detail.

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm --examine --scan
ARRAY /dev/md1 UUID=79607c5c:84632425:12f9938a:7c5726ef
   spares=1

```Because it seems to be inactive I tried
```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm -R /dev/md127
mdadm: failed to run array /dev/md127: Input/output error

```

I'm having trouble getting my head around all the different flavours of Raid (Md raid, SOftware raid, FakeRaid etc etc) in existence nowadays and the way it all works in Linux i.e. mdadm.
I ckecked the man pages for mdadm but found the information a bit to verbose.. Most of the time I have trouble understanding the output given by mdadm, and I don't know where to find information on these messages... 
 
Is there a place where there is a comprehensive guide/ explanation of the working concept of Linux software Raid and mdadm in general??

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-05
Just flaundering at this point ;-) I did this fdisk... doesn't look good or is this command not to be applied on disks that are part of a Raid array?
I did this to try and find out the type of filesystem, which would help searching for a solution on the net.

```


ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo fdisk -l /dev/sdc1

Disk /dev/sdc1: 2000.4 GB, 2000398884352 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 243201 cylinders, total 3907029071 sectors
Units = sectors of 1 * 512 = 512 bytes
Sector size (logical/physical): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
I/O size (minimum/optimal): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
Disk identifier: 0x00000000

Disk /dev/sdc1 doesn't contain a valid partition table

```

---

### Post by steeldriver on 2012-12-05
Hi again, 

I think that's normal for fdisk - let's take a step back because I'm confused about what your status is at this point

- are both disks attached or only one? 

- what is the current output from mdadm -E on each of the original RAID members?

Because it was RAID 1 (simple mirror) it *may* be possible to mount a single member, however you'd need to go about that differently (no mdadm - just probe the underlying fstype and attempt to mount the partition)

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-05
I tried this command I found in an article on mdadm(?) and now I'm finding partitions!?!
```


ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo sfdisk -l /dev/sdc

WARNING: GPT (GUID Partition Table) detected on '/dev/sdc'! The util sfdisk doesn't support GPT. Use GNU Parted.


Disk /dev/sdc: 243201 cylinders, 255 heads, 63 sectors/track
Units = cylinders of 8225280 bytes, blocks of 1024 bytes, counting from 0

   Device Boot Start     End   #cyls    #blocks   Id  System
/dev/sdc1          0+ 243201- 243202- 1953514583+  ee  GPT
        start: (c,h,s) expected (0,0,2) found (0,0,1)
/dev/sdc2          0       -       0          0    0  Empty
/dev/sdc3          0       -       0          0    0  Empty
/dev/sdc4          0       -       0          0    0  Empty


```Ok.. taking a step back...

> 
Because it was RAID 1 (simple mirror) it *may* be possible to mount a  single member, however you'd need to go about that differently (no mdadm  - just probe the underlying fstype and attempt to mount the partition)
I prefer working with just the one drive.. However I will do what is best, I'm presuming that it's not possible or easy at this point to restore the array to get the Nas working again. There's not a lot of data on it (it was new and I didn't "trust" it yet). So I can store the data for the moment , and decide what to do on a more permanent basis.

For the entire second disk
```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm -E /dev/sdc
/dev/sdc:

```For the partition
```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm -E /dev/sdc1

   2     2       8        1        2      spare   /dev/sda1

Back again... same for the first disk in the array
```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm -E /dev/sdc
/dev/sdc:
   MBR Magic : aa55
Partition[0] :   3907029167 sectors at            1 (type ee)
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm -E /dev/sdc1
/dev/sdc1:
          Magic : a92b4efc
        Version : 0.90.00

---

### Post by steeldriver on 2012-12-05
It *may* be possible to assemble and mount the array in a degraded state using just one of the original array members - however I think you would need to jump through some hoops (possibly formally marking the other member(s) as 'failed' and/or 'removed'; or actually re-creating the array with --assume-clean instead of assembling) - that's really beyond my comfort level, if you want to go that route I would suggest a bat signal or PM to rubylaser who seems to be the mdadm wizard in these parts

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-05
Hi Steeldriver lots of thanks ,
 I spied on you a little bit (sorry!!) and by looking at a thread you were looking at, I found something to try..... finally I got some output that made some sense to me .. in general the trouble is, I'm not sure what's going on with the NAS (runs some distribution of Linux I guess), what is the technology behind it. And Linux in my case Ubuntu, of which I have  practically "zero"knowledge", is the easiest way to take care of the situation.

At this point I would like to attain some understanding of the techn. at work here. As it seems that "the universe" has created this opportunity for me! ;-) And, a similar situation will almost surely arise in the future! Hopefully you guys/girls will give me a hand!I'll bring the snacks&drinks!

OK, so I tried
```


Disk 1
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo blkid /dev/md127
/dev/md127: UUID="1f607c88-35f2-4387-b38a-2a4217e47a06" TYPE="xfs" 
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm --detail /dev/md127
/dev/md127:
        Version : 0.90
  Creation Time : Sat Dec  3 18:08:49 2011
     Raid Level : raid1
     Array Size : 1953514432 (1863.02 GiB 2000.40 GB)
  Used Dev Size : 1953514432 (1863.02 GiB 2000.40 GB)
   Raid Devices : 2
  Total Devices : 1
Preferred Minor : 127
    Persistence : Superblock is persistent

    Update Time : Sat Dec  1 00:38:02 2012
          State : clean, degraded 
 Active Devices : 1
Working Devices : 1
 Failed Devices : 0
  Spare Devices : 0

           UUID : 79607c5c:84632425:12f9938a:7c5726ef
         Events : 0.302

    Number   Major   Minor   RaidDevice State
       0       0        0        0      removed
       1       8       33        1      active sync   /dev/sdc1

```Now the bottom lines make a lot more sense!! And the blkid gives me the type of filesystem, which is also nice to know!
Btw, What is the meaning of those Minor and Major columns?

```

Disk 2
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo blkid /dev/md127
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mdadm --detail /dev/md127
mdadm: md device /dev/md127 does not appear to be active.

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ cat /proc/mdstat
Personalities : [linear] [multipath] [raid0] [raid1] [raid6] [raid5] [raid4] [raid10] 
md127 : inactive sdc1[2](S)
      1953514432 blocks

```So when I switch disks... the array can be identified but not manipulated? So the disks are not the same? Then it isn't really a mirror, right?? Or is this because they might be out of sync?

In any case I get the feeling that if I would try the operations to mount the array with the first disk, instead of the second which I was doing, then I might not get the same errors, ie it might work...

I see that on both disks it says "Events:302" what does this signify? Is there a way to read these events?
It would be nice to find out what initially went wrong!

Maybe a stupid question, would I be able to put the disks back in the Nas and do all this stuff via telnet or similar? Just because I really rather wouldn't put the disks in my pc. The Nas has  a "build-in" Linux os , no?

Definitely time to go to bed!!!!

---

### Post by rubylaser on 2012-12-06
Hello,

I've tried to read through your thread to get up to speed.  First, I'm sorry you're going through this.  Potential data loss is never fun.  Since this is RAID1, you can easily mount any partition on either disk without needing to assemble the mdadm RAID1.

At this point, your metadata on each disk are showing completely different entries (and event counters), so it would be safer for your data to treat these disks separately at this point.  The first thing I'd do is run a smart test on each disk and make sure they are both healthly.

```
sudo -i
apt-get install smartmontools parted
smartctl -a /dev/sdb
smartctl -a /dev/sdc
```
Please post those results back (I'm unsure at this point if these are the correct disks, but adjust accordingly).

Next, since the disks contain GPT partitiona, you'll want to use parted to examine them. You should be able to use parted to identify the filesystem used on the partitions.

```
parted -l
```
The output should look something like this (I have more disks that you, but you'll get the idea0.
```
root@fileserver:~# parted -l
Model: ATA OWC Mercury Extr (scsi)
Disk /dev/sda: 50.0GB
Sector size (logical/physical): 512B/512B
Partition Table: msdos

Number  Start   End     Size    Type      File system     Flags
 1      1049kB  47.9GB  47.9GB  primary   ext4            boot
 2      47.9GB  50.0GB  2144MB  extended
 5      47.9GB  50.0GB  2144MB  logical   linux-swap(v1)


Model: ATA ST2000DL003-9VT1 (scsi)
Disk /dev/sdb: 2000GB
Sector size (logical/physical): 512B/512B
Partition Table: gpt

Number  Start   End     Size    File system  Name     Flags
 1      1049kB  2000GB  2000GB  ext4         primary


Model: ATA Hitachi HDS5C302 (scsi)
Disk /dev/sdc: 2000GB
Sector size (logical/physical): 512B/512B
Partition Table: gpt

Number  Start   End     Size    File system  Name     Flags
 1      1049kB  2000GB  2000GB  ext4         primary


Model: ATA ST2000DL003-9VT1 (scsi)
Disk /dev/sdd: 2000GB
Sector size (logical/physical): 512B/512B
Partition Table: gpt

Number  Start   End     Size    File system  Name     Flags
 1      1049kB  2000GB  2000GB  ext4         primary


Model: ATA ST32000542AS (scsi)
Disk /dev/sde: 2000GB
Sector size (logical/physical): 512B/512B
Partition Table: gpt

Number  Start   End     Size    File system  Name     Flags
 1      1049kB  2000GB  2000GB  ext4         primary


Model: ATA ST2000DL003-9VT1 (scsi)
Disk /dev/sdf: 2000GB
Sector size (logical/physical): 512B/4096B
Partition Table: gpt

Number  Start   End     Size    File system  Name     Flags
 1      1049kB  2000GB  2000GB  ext4         primary
```

Please post those results back here.  Finally, if your disks are healthly and they have a recognizeable filesystem on them, it should be as easy as something like this to mount them.

```
mkdir /mnt/sdc1
mount /dev/sdc1 /mnt/sdc1
```

Your disk should be mounted at /mnt/sdc1 now.
```
cd /mnt/sdc1
ls
```

---

---

### Post by rubylaser on 2012-12-06
Both disks look okay, so you should be able to read from either one.  They both use XFS for the filesystem, so you'll need to mount them with the XFS mount option.

```
sudo -i
mkdir -p /mnt/sdb1
mount -t xfs /dev/sdb1 /mnt/sdb1
cd /mnt/sdb1
ls
```

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-06
Hi :p I'm currently copying my data to a third Hd !!! Thanks a million, people!!
 
It seems it (the completew array) mounted automatic as soon as I started Ubuntu... I just didn't see it there on the left bottom part of the screen. I forgot how much stuff I already put on there!! Phew, really ... THANKS you guys!!!!
 
But after copying I would like to try some stuff to expand my knowledge.. I guess the first thing to try now is putting the drives back in the NAS. After that , I will definitely try the above, RubyL.
 
But now that the array is already running. Wouldn't it be better to first try them together in the Nas, before I try to mount them as a single unit (not an array right?) ?
 
Is there any reason (on the filesystem level) why that wouldn't work?
Don't I have to stop the array first?
Can I ascertain in some way, what went wrong all those months ago?

---

### Post by rubylaser on 2012-12-06
Great, I'm glad that it's working :) Based on the couple UDMA errors in your SMART data (typically indicates a bad SATA cable, but in a NAS, it's likely you don't have cables), the communication was interrupted and the disk was removed from the array in the metadata on the second disk.  Since then, they have been out of sync.  You could force assemble the array, but once your data is backed up, I'd overwrite each disk with zeroes, and start over from scratch.

---

So now I can try to get to the data on the second disk. Which previously, I wasn't able to do (can't read superblock, array seems to be inactive, etc), wish me luck!!

So I recreated my previous situation, with the difference that the second disk doesn't have errors anymore, no bad sector. Should only make things easier!

Attempt to attach the second drive from a drive Raid1 array comprised of 2 Hds.

Scenario 2

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-07
**[SIZE=4]Test Scenario 1[/SIZE]**
 
 
The mount directories where created by me in advance..
 
First try , as suggested by RubyLaser [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=12391639&postcount=24](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=12391639&postcount=24)
```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo -i
root@ubuntu:~# mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/sdb1
mount: unknown filesystem type 'linux_raid_member'
root@ubuntu:~# mount -t xfs /dev/sdb1 /mnt/sdb1
mount: /dev/sdb1 already mounted or /mnt/sdb1 busy
 

```Second try, as suggested by Steeldriver [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=12388069&postcount=11](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=12388069&postcount=11)
```

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mount -oro /dev/sdb /mnt/raid
mount: /dev/sdb already mounted or /mnt/raid busy
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mount -oro /dev/sdb1 /mnt/raid
mount: unknown filesystem type 'linux_raid_member'
root@ubuntu:~# mount -oro -t xfs /dev/sdb1 /mnt/raid      <<Added &tried later for full measure
mount: /dev/sdb1 already mounted or /mnt/raid busy
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mount -oro /dev/md127 /mnt/raid
mount: /dev/md127: can't read superblock

### Post by rubylaser on 2012-12-07
Since you're using the Desktop install, it sounds like it tried to automount your array.  You should probably take a look at the output of these.

```
sudo -i
cat /proc/mdstat
mount
```

---

Luckily I already looked at mdstat at the start of Scenario1:
```
 
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ cat /proc/mdstat

md127 : inactive sdb1[2](S)
      1953514432 blocks
 
unused devices: <none>

```

root@ubuntu:~# mount

sysfs on /sys type sysfs (rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev)

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$

```For my scenario1 it doesn't really matter because I want to mount is just as /dev/sdb1... but I would have liked to give the "mount" in exactly the state that you where commenting on... SORRY.. for Scenario 2 I'll restore the array using the first disk later..

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-07
Ok, I was able to restart=assemble the array after all 
 
```

root@ubuntu:~# mdadm --examine /dev/sdb1
/dev/sdb1:
          Magic : a92b4efc
        Version : 0.90.00
           UUID : 79607c5c:84632425:12f9938a:7c5726ef
  Creation Time : Sat Dec  3 18:08:49 2011
     Raid Level : raid1
  Used Dev Size : 1953514432 (1863.02 GiB 2000.40 GB)
     Array Size : 1953514432 (1863.02 GiB 2000.40 GB)
   Raid Devices : 2
  Total Devices : 2
Preferred Minor : 127
 
    Update Time : Fri Dec  7 03:32:42 2012
          State : clean
 Active Devices : 1
Working Devices : 2
 Failed Devices : 0
  Spare Devices : 1
       Checksum : 4a4b9f12 - correct
         Events : 372
 
 
      Number   Major   Minor   RaidDevice State
this     2       8       49        2      spare
 
   0     0       0        0        0      removed
   1     1       8       17        1      active sync   /dev/sdb1
   2     2       8       49        2      spare
root@ubuntu:~# mdadm /dev/md127 --assemble -u 79607c5c:84632425:12f9938a:7c5726ef
root@ubuntu:~# 
 

```I think that fixed me stopping the array, right?
So I'll do "mount" again:
```

root@ubuntu:~# mount
/cow on / type overlayfs (rw)
proc on /proc type proc (rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev)
sysfs on /sys type sysfs (rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev)
udev on /dev type devtmpfs (rw,mode=0755)
devpts on /dev/pts type devpts (rw,noexec,nosuid,gid=5,mode=0620)
tmpfs on /run type tmpfs (rw,noexec,nosuid,size=10%,mode=0755)
/dev/sdc1 on /cdrom type vfat (rw,relatime,fmask=0022,dmask=0022,codepage=cp437,iocharset=iso8859-1,shortname=mixed,errors=remount-ro)
/dev/loop0 on /rofs type squashfs (ro,noatime)
none on /sys/fs/fuse/connections type fusectl (rw)
none on /sys/kernel/debug type debugfs (rw)
none on /sys/kernel/security type securityfs (rw)
tmpfs on /tmp type tmpfs (rw,nosuid,nodev)
none on /run/lock type tmpfs (rw,noexec,nosuid,nodev,size=5242880)

Strange!!!! There seem to be 3 devices in the array??? And in the first output there's information that's contradictory to second output.. Look at the lines in red!!.. the list..
 
Can you explain this?

root@ubuntu:~# mdadm --query -D /dev/md127
/dev/md127:

mdadm: --examine/-E cannot be given with -D

```

---

### Post by steeldriver on 2012-12-07
I don't think you can rely on the sda / sdb / sdc device designators being consistent - especially if you are swapping devices in and out between boots (they are generated on the fly - hence why UUIDs are used in modern fstabs). [In fact the designators you are seeing for the active + spare devices in mdadm -E may just reflect how they were labeled in the original NAS.]

What exactly are you trying to do now? were you able to recover your data as rubylaser suggested by mounting the 'good' drive's underlying xfs partition directly?

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-07
Hi Steeldriver!!!
Thread is marked SOLVED isn't it??!! :P Thnx. for your input
 
If you start reading at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=12391818&postcount=27](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=12391818&postcount=27) I think it will be clear, what I'm trying to do..
 
My comments above.. about the strange output. The names (sdx) are not what I'm focusing on.. but the Minor+Major+State as seen in the array query as opposed to that in the disk examination (You have to scroll to see all the red text). If I'm not mistaking in the array query sdb1 (the attached disk) is device2 and in the disk examination it's device1 !! I base the identification on the match in the MinorMajor numbers though. But madmin -E says "**this **Minor 2 Major 48" which would be the device your examing no? It's clear as mud! ;-)
 
I like that UUID, it seemed to allow me to assemble the array which I wasn't able to do with the array name.
And also strange, the number of devices: 0, 1 and 2 which makes three devices, unless it's: The array + 2 disks, but if that's the case why does it say "removed"?
 
Status
My data is save on a third medium about 60Gb saved (luckily I had double redundancy on most of my data)!
The Nas, I think, has failures in the cables/connections in the second bay.
When I attached both disks at once to my pc, the array mounted automatic and resychronised, there were some errors on the second disk, but they were fixed as soon as I powered up I guess.
With only the primary disk attached I can access the data, get's automounted also.
With only the second disk I still haven't been able to get anywhere (no more errors on it).

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-08
I'm giving up on Scenario1 I read a lot in the last hours. I tried al the suggested solutions (in threads,manuals, tutorials). I haven't been able to find anyone that was able to do this successfully, as an example..
[http://serverfault.com/questions/381470/how-do-you-mount-a-raid1-disk-as-a-normal-disk](http://serverfault.com/questions/381470/how-do-you-mount-a-raid1-disk-as-a-normal-disk)

[SIZE=4]**Solution for this thread**[/SIZE]

[SIZE=3]Attached both disks to a pc running Ubuntu Live USB, array get's auto-mounted and re-synchronised.[/SIZE]

---

### Post by RED666 on 2012-12-08
I think this might have something to do with my problem; mounting just the mirror as a normal disk or starting the |(or another) array with just the mirror.
 
> 
**Known problems**
 
A common error when creating RAID devices is that the dmraid-driver has taken control of all the devices that are to be used in the new RAID device. Error-messages like this will occur:
mdadm: Cannot open /dev/sdb1: Device or resource busy
Typically, the solution to this problem involves adding the "nodmraid" kernel parameter to the boot loader config. Another way this error can present itself is if the device mapper has its way with the drives. Issue 'dmsetup table' see if the drive in question is listed. 'dmsetup remove <drive id>' will remove the drive from device mapper and the "Device or resource busy" error will go away as well.
*[SIZE=1]http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mdadm#RAID_already_running[/SIZE]*
 

 
And this:
> 
 
From Linux kernel version 2.6.10, **md** provides support for two different formats of metadata, and other formats can be added. Prior to this release, only one format is supported. 
 
The common format -- **known as version 0.90** -- has a superblock that is 4K long and is written into a 64K aligned block that starts at least 64K and less than 128K from the end of the device (i.e. to get the address of the superblock round the size of the device down to a multiple of 64K and then subtract 64K). The available size of each device is the amount of space before the super block, so between 64K and 128K is lost when a device in incorporated into an MD array. **This superblock stores multi-byte fields in a processor-dependent manner, so arrays cannot easily be moved between computers with different processors**.
 
The new format -- known as version 1 -- has a superblock that is normally 1K long, but can be longer. It is normally stored between 8K and 12K from the end of the device, on a 4K boundary, though variations can be stored at the start of the device (version 1.1) or 4K from the start of the device (version 1.2). This metadata format stores multibyte data in a processor-independent format and supports up to hundreds of component devices (**version 0.90 only supports 28**). 
 
[*http://linux.die.net/man/4/md*]("http://linux.die.net/man/4/md")


---
